description: Italy, Venice, Piazza San Marco, the “Caffè Florian” under the arcades of the “Procuratie Nuove” at the San Marco Square was founded by the Cafétier Floriano Francesconi in December 1720 under the name "Venezia Trionfante", Triumphant Venice, but the Venetian only called the Café by the first name of the owner & so it was changed later into “Caffè Florian”.   The Café used to be the place to meet for Italy’s patriots, artists & intellectuals as well as international well-known personalities; today’s patrons are basically tourists.  The lagoon city hides many picturesque places offside the tourist roads. title: Castraure (baby Artichokes) to die for
tags: venice wine market fishmarket venezia artichoke rialto foodie carciofi vegetablemarket dalluva castraure
description: The best of the best, baby artichokes are called "castraure" (pronounced cass-trah-OO-ray, and literally translate as "castrated") and are the most tender. They’re the side shoots of the plant, nipped young and before the fuzzy choke has developed. You boil/braise/steam these babies, drizzle a little olive oil or butter on them, and pop the whole thing in your mouth.

title: Phot.Venice.Basilica.La.Salute.041024.6645.jpg
tags: china plaza city travel venice india streets reflection berlin art bar night mall germany munich münchen square noche calle nikon asia strada place market nacht zurich hamburg chinese beijing ciudad places historic coolpix nightlife d200 rue venezia nuit venedig städte ville citta d300 strasen plätze colorphotoaward flickrstruereflection1
description: Italy, Venice, “Basilica di Santa Maria della Salute”, commonly known simply as “La Salute”, is one of the largest churches of Venice & has the status of a minor basilica. It stands at the junction between the Canale Grande & the “Bacino di San Marco” on the lagoon. In October 1630, the Venetian Senate decreed that if the city was delivered from the currently raging plague that had killed about a third of Venice's population, then a new church would be built and dedicated to the Virgin Mary.  The city was so delivered & Baldassare Longhena, then only 26 years old, was selected to design the new church. It was consecrated in 1681, the year before Longhena's death & completed in 1687.

description: Italy, Venice, Gondoliere, what would the charming Venice be without its Gondolas, Gondolieri & decadent buildings?  There are 425 Gondolas in Venice with a license, which is hard to get & stays normally in the Family. A license for a Gondoliere can cost up to 500.000 €, on the other hand, a Gondoliere makes between 10 & 15 thousand € a month during main season.  The lagoon city hides many picturesque places offside the tourist roads. If the buildings would be straight, painted & well maintained, I think Venice without this patina, the gondolas, channels, little bridges & the at all-time busy Canal Grande; these main ingredients altogether express the romantic charm of Venice.    👉 One World one Dream, 🙏...Danke, Xièxie 谢谢, Thanks, Gracias, Merci, Grazie, Obrigado, Arigatô, Dhanyavad, Chokrane to you & over  16  million visits in my photostream with countless motivating comments

title: Canale Grande
tags: street travel italien venice italy building architecture restaurant boat alley market balcony magic gothic perspective ponte worldwide gondola markt venedig rialto sanmarco mittelmeer gondeln dogepalace panasoniclumixdmcfz18
description: The Grand Canal is a canal in Venice, Italy. It forms one of the major water-traffic corridors in the city. Public transport is provided by water buses and private water taxis, but many tourists visit it by gondola. At one end the canal leads into the lagoon near Santa Lucia railway station and the other end leads into Saint Mark Basin: in between it makes a large S-shape through the central districts ("sestieri") of Venice. It is 3,800 m long, 30-90 m wide, with an average depth of five meters.

description: Italy, Venice, Piazza San Marco, the “Caffè Florian” under the arcades of the “Procuratie Nuove” at the San Marco Square was founded by the Cafétier Floriano Francesconi in December 1720 under the name "Venezia Trionfante", Triumphant Venice, but the Venetian only called the Café by the first name of the owner & so it was changed later into “Caffè Florian”.   The Café used to be the place to meet for Italy’s patriots, artists & intellectuals as well as international well-known personalities; today’s patrons are basically tourists.  The lagoon city hides many picturesque places offside the tourist roads. If the venetian buildings would be straight, painted & well maintained, without their unique buildings & their patina, the gondolas, channels, little bridges & the at all-time busy Canale Grande; these main ingredients altogether express the romantic charm of Venice,…I think.   👉 One World one Dream, 🙏...Danke, Xièxie 谢谢, Thanks, Gracias, Merci, Grazie, Obrigado, Arigatô, Dhanyavad, Chokrane to you & over  13  million visits in my photostream with countless motivating comments

tags: italien venice italy fruit italia market 1994 sales venezia frucht marché venedig italie velence augusztus gemüse venezzia nyár gyümölcs piac olaszország zöldség eladó
description: Market next to the Rialto Bridge in Venice, Italy. We spent a day in this marvelous town in late August 1994, taking a detour on our way home from Western Austria (my wife had a one-month scholarship there at the university). We walked from the station to the city center and took the boat back, enjoyed the wonderful buildings and the air of the ancient city state.  Piac a Rialto-híd mellett Velencében - egy teljes napot töltöttünk ebben a csodálatos városban 1994. augusztus végén. Egyhónapos innsbrucki tartózkodásunkról hazafelé tettünk egy kis kitérőt, vonattal reggel érkeztünk és éjszaka távoztunk, közben besétáltunk a központba, majd hajóval mentünk vissza az állomásra. Nagyon élveztük a város szép régi épületeit és a valaha hatalmas városállam hangulatát.

title: The Rialto Market, Venice, Italy
tags: from bridge venice italy church retail america fire for boat tv concert san italia market live di campo were 1983 della warwick venezia merchant portuguese polo destroyed dei dionne wholesale rialto sonnets greengrocery giacomo pescheria tedeschi fondaco 1514 sestiere erberia
description: Rialto is an area of the San Polo sestiere of Venice, Italy, known for its markets and for the Rialto Bridge.  The area was settled by the ninth century, when a small area in the middle of the Realtine Islands either side of the Rio Businiacus was known as the Rivoaltus, the "high bank". Soon, the Businiacus became known as the Grand Canal, and the district became the Rialto, referring only to the area on the left bank.  The Rialto became an important district in 1097, when Venice's market moved there, and in the following century a boat bridge was set up across the Grand Canal providing access to it. This was soon replaced by the Rialto Bridge.   The market grew, both as a retail and as a wholesale market. Warehouses were built, including the famous Fondaco dei Tedeschi on the other side of the bridge. Meanwhile, shops selling luxury goods, banks and insurance agencies appeared and the city's tax offices were located in the area. The city's abattoir was also in the Rialto.  Most of the buildings in the Rialto were destroyed in a fire in 1514, the sole survivor being the Church of San Giacomo di Rialto, while the rest of the area was gradually rebuilt. The Fabriche Vecchie dates from this period, while the Fabbriche Nuove is only slightly more recent, dating from 1553. The statue Il Gobbo di Rialto was also sculpted in the sixteenth century.  The area is still a busy retail quarter, with the daily Erberia greengrocery market, and the fish market on the Campo della Pescheria.  The Rialto is also mentioned in works of literature, notably in The Merchant of Venice (Act I, Scene III) and in Elizabeth Barrett Browning's Sonnets from the Portuguese (Sonnet 19).  Dionne Warwick performed here her first Live TV Concert in 1983 for America.
